inline_review:
commit 4f3b06e8a44832fea7054c1a08defab2532bf3ca
Author: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>

memblock: warn when freeing reserved memory before memory map is initialized

This commit adds a warning when attempting to free reserved memory before the
memory map is fully initialized by deferred_init_memmap(). This aims to
prevent access to uninitialized struct pages which can cause crashes.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/e5d5a1105d90ee1e7fe7eafaed2ed03bbad0c46b.camel@kernel.crashing.org/

> diff --git a/mm/memblock.c b/mm/memblock.c
> index bd5758ff07f2..780e70d4971a 100644
> --- a/mm/memblock.c
> +++ b/mm/memblock.c
> @@ -896,6 +896,11 @@ static unsigned long __free_reserved_area(phys_addr_t start, phys_addr_t end,
>  {
>  	unsigned long pages = 0, pfn;
>  
> +	if (deferred_pages_enabled()) {
> +		WARN(1, "Cannot free reserved memory because of deferred initialization of the memory map");
> +		return 0;
> +	}
> +

Does this global check block freeing memory that is already fully
initialized, such as memory in ZONE_DMA or ZONE_NORMAL?

Since __free_reserved_area() returns 0 when this triggers, the pages are
not given to the buddy allocator. However, the caller memblock_phys_free()
does not check this return value and proceeds to call
memblock_remove_range(). Does this result in a permanent memory leak for
early valid frees?

Would it be better to check if the specific PFN range is actually deferred,
rather than applying a global restriction?

Could this also leave direct callers of free_reserved_page() unprotected?

For example, architecture-specific initialization code (like in
arch/sparc/kernel/leon_smp.c) might call free_reserved_page() during early
boot before page_alloc_init_late().

Would it be safer to place this validation directly inside
free_reserved_page() to ensure all paths are covered?

>  	for_each_valid_pfn(pfn, PFN_UP(start), PFN_DOWN(end)) {
>  		struct page *page = pfn_to_page(pfn);
>  		void *direct_map_addr;

Target Commit:
commit 4f3b06e8a44832fea7054c1a08defab2532bf3ca
Author: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>
Date:   Wed Mar 18 10:58:27 2026 +0000

    memblock: warn when freeing reserved memory before memory map is initialized
    
    When CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT is enabled, freeing of reserved
    memory before the memory map is fully initialized in deferred_init_memmap()
    would cause access to uninitialized struct pages and may crash when
    accessing spurious list pointers, like was recently discovered during
    discussion about memory leaks in x86 EFI code [1].
    
    The trace below is from an attempt to call free_reserved_page() before
    page_alloc_init_late():
    
    [    0.076840] BUG: unable to handle page fault for address: ffffce1a005a0788
    [    0.078226] #PF: supervisor read access in kernel mode
    [    0.078226] #PF: error_code(0x0000) - not-present page
    [    0.078226] PGD 0 P4D 0
    [    0.078226] Oops: Oops: 0000 [#1] PREEMPT SMP NOPTI
    [    0.078226] CPU: 0 UID: 0 PID: 0 Comm: swapper/0 Not tainted 6.12.68-92.123.amzn2023.x86_64 #1
    [    0.078226] Hardware name: Amazon EC2 t3a.nano/, BIOS 1.0 10/16/2017
    [    0.078226] RIP: 0010:__list_del_entry_valid_or_report+0x32/0xb0
    ...
    [    0.078226]  __free_one_page+0x170/0x520
    [    0.078226]  free_pcppages_bulk+0x151/0x1e0
    [    0.078226]  free_unref_page_commit+0x263/0x320
    [    0.078226]  free_unref_page+0x2c8/0x5b0
    [    0.078226]  ? srso_return_thunk+0x5/0x5f
    [    0.078226]  free_reserved_page+0x1c/0x30
    [    0.078226]  memblock_free_late+0x6c/0xc0
    
    Currently there are not many callers of free_reserved_area() and they all
    appear to be at the right timings.
    
    Still, in order to protect against problematic code moves or additions of
    new callers add a warning that will inform that reserved pages cannot be
    freed until the memory map is fully initialized.
    
    [1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/e5d5a1105d90ee1e7fe7eafaed2ed03bbad0c46b.camel@kernel.crashing.org/
    
    Signed-off-by: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>

diff --git a/mm/internal.h b/mm/internal.h
index cb0af847d7d9..f60c1edb2e02 100644
--- a/mm/internal.h
+++ b/mm/internal.h
@@ -1233,7 +1233,17 @@ static inline void vunmap_range_noflush(unsigned long start, unsigned long end)
 #ifdef CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT
 DECLARE_STATIC_KEY_TRUE(deferred_pages);
 
+static inline bool deferred_pages_enabled(void)
+{
+	return static_branch_unlikely(&deferred_pages);
+}
+
 bool __init deferred_grow_zone(struct zone *zone, unsigned int order);
+#else
+static inline bool deferred_pages_enabled(void)
+{
+	return false;
+}
 #endif /* CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT */
 
 void init_deferred_page(unsigned long pfn, int nid);
diff --git a/mm/memblock.c b/mm/memblock.c
index bd5758ff07f2..780e70d4971a 100644
--- a/mm/memblock.c
+++ b/mm/memblock.c
@@ -896,6 +896,11 @@ static unsigned long __free_reserved_area(phys_addr_t start, phys_addr_t end,
 {
 	unsigned long pages = 0, pfn;
 
+	if (deferred_pages_enabled()) {
+		WARN(1, "Cannot free reserved memory because of deferred initialization of the memory map");
+		return 0;
+	}
+
 	for_each_valid_pfn(pfn, PFN_UP(start), PFN_DOWN(end)) {
 		struct page *page = pfn_to_page(pfn);
 		void *direct_map_addr;
diff --git a/mm/page_alloc.c b/mm/page_alloc.c
index df3d61253001..9ac47bab2ea7 100644
--- a/mm/page_alloc.c
+++ b/mm/page_alloc.c
@@ -331,11 +331,6 @@ int page_group_by_mobility_disabled __read_mostly;
  */
 DEFINE_STATIC_KEY_TRUE(deferred_pages);
 
-static inline bool deferred_pages_enabled(void)
-{
-	return static_branch_unlikely(&deferred_pages);
-}
-
 /*
  * deferred_grow_zone() is __init, but it is called from
  * get_page_from_freelist() during early boot until deferred_pages permanently
@@ -348,11 +343,6 @@ _deferred_grow_zone(struct zone *zone, unsigned int order)
 	return deferred_grow_zone(zone, order);
 }
 #else
-static inline bool deferred_pages_enabled(void)
-{
-	return false;
-}
-
 static inline bool _deferred_grow_zone(struct zone *zone, unsigned int order)
 {
 	return false;

    "thought": "**Analysis of the Reserved Memory Freeing Patch**

Okay, so this patch is adding a safety net around freeing reserved memory when `CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT` is enabled. My first thought is: why is this even necessary? Let's break this down.

The core of the problem seems to be that if we try to free reserved memory *before* the memory map is fully initialized (i.e., before `deferred_init_memmap()` runs), we risk accessing uninitialized `struct page` entries, and thus a crash from accessing spurious list pointers. The author is suggesting a warning, and a bail-out from freeing the memory, in this case.

The key part is the introduction of `deferred_pages_enabled()`, which checks the state of a static key, `deferred_pages`. This static key is initialized to TRUE, meaning the check will be true until `page_alloc_init_late()` is called, when it is disabled.

The patch's logic is this: if `deferred_pages_enabled()` returns true (i.e. the memory map isn't ready), `__free_reserved_area` will emit a warning and return 0 (no pages freed). My first question is: Is this the right behavior, or does it cause a memory leak if some code is relying on this at that early boot stage? It is intended to prevent a crash, as the commit message says, but it *leaks* memory.

My main concern revolves around whether this is truly a complete solution. The commit message states that existing callers "appear to be at the right timings". However, the backtrace suggests that `memblock_free_late` is called *before* `page_alloc_init_late`, but this patch *only* modifies `__free_reserved_area`. The patch **doesn't** seem to affect `free_reserved_page`, which is the function that is directly called by `memblock_free_late`. Is this truly preventing the crash?

Specifically, the patch modifies `__free_reserved_area`, but the commit says the crash is from `memblock_free_late` calling `free_reserved_page`! This patch doesn't prevent this!
This means that `memblock_free_late` might bypass the safety check entirely, as it calls `free_reserved_page` directly. Did the author mean that `free_reserved_page` is called from `__free_reserved_area`? No, because `__free_reserved_area` is *not* called by `free_reserved_page` as per the text. This is an oversight.

So, in summary:

*   The patch adds a warning and prevents memory freeing *only* in `__free_reserved_area` if `deferred_pages` is enabled.
*   The memory is leaked instead of freeing when the static key is enabled.
*   However, the commit message implies a crash is due to `memblock_free_late` calling `free_reserved_page`, and this safety net doesn't cover that case. So, the patch seems ineffective in its goal to prevent a crash, while it may be helpful if the code path is through `__free_reserved_area`.
*   The assumption is there are no problematic callers, other than the crash reported, as per the author's comment "Currently there are not many callers of free\_reserved\_area() and they all appear to be at the right timings."
*   There's a potential memory leak now with this logic.

I think there needs to be a deeper investigation. This needs some further analysis to really solve this problem!

Okay, so here's the situation I'm looking at: We're dealing with how the kernel handles freeing reserved memory during the boot process. The core concern is the timing of when memory is actually freed versus when the mechanisms for managing it are initialized. Specifically, the question is how `memblock_phys_free()` (and by extension, the now-obsoleted `memblock_free_late`) interacts with `deferred_pages` and slab allocation during early boot.

The kernel starts with `start_kernel()`, which leads to `mm_core_init()`. This is important because `mm_core_init()` calls both `mem_init()` and then `kmem_cache_init()`. The crucial bit here is the *order*: `mem_init()` happens *before* `kmem_cache_init()`. This is important because `kmem_cache_init()` initializes the slab allocator and sets `slab_is_available()`.

Now, `memblock_phys_free()` is called in `mem_init()`.  However, because `slab_is_available()` is still `false` at that stage, it doesn't call `__free_reserved_area()` directly. Instead, it just removes the memory from `memblock.reserved`. This isn't a problem, because early on, this reserved memory isn't actually managed by the buddy allocator yet.

The potential problem lies *after* `kmem_cache_init()` but *before* `page_alloc_init_late()`.  This region includes `do_pre_smp_initcalls()`, `rest_init()`, and things that happen inside `kernel_init_freeable()` before `page_alloc_init_late()` is called.  This is where the patch kicks in because after the `slab` is initialized, the `memblock_phys_free()` will call `__free_reserved_area()`. And if `deferred_pages_enabled()` is true (which it is at this stage), it will trigger a warning and prevent the memory from being actually freed. The memory will be leaked.

I need to confirm if there are any early initcalls calling `memblock_phys_free()`. I saw a call in `arch/x86/kernel/setup.c` within `reserve_ima_kexec_buffer()`. But wait, that's called in `setup_arch()`, which occurs *before* `mm_core_init()`, so there's no problem.  `slab_is_available()` is still `false`. So this is fine.

The commit text mentions a crash, traced back to `memblock_free_late`. But I don't see `memblock_free_late` in the source! It seems the author added a guard to `__free_reserved_area()` as a safety measure. The patch doesn't modify `memblock_free_late()`. So the crash must be from a separate issue entirely.

I need to circle back to `memblock_free_late`. The author said they found it when discussing memory leaks in x86 EFI code. Maybe `memblock_free_late` is a different name for `memblock_phys_free`? No, the trace clearly says `memblock_free_late`. I'll have to grep for this function again to find it. The crash must be from *something* calling `free_reserved_page()` too early.

The key takeaway is that the patch is safeguarding against unintentional memory leaks due to improper timing of calling `memblock_phys_free()` after the slab allocator is ready, and before the `page_alloc_init_late()` stage.

If that's the case, then all reserved pages *should* have valid `struct page` entries already, right?

But the commit message introduces a red flag, citing "access to uninitialized struct pages" if reserved memory is freed before the memory map is fully initialized. How can those pages be uninitialized if `reserve_bootmem_region` *should* have initialized them? Ah, I remember - `memmap_init_reserved_pages()` skips regions marked `MEMBLOCK_RSRV_NOINIT`! Okay, that explains one possibility. Are there any other reasons this might happen?

If a region is `MEMBLOCK_RSRV_NOINIT`, it's skipped by *both* `reserve_bootmem_region` and `deferred_init_memmap_chunk`. That means a `MEMBLOCK_RSRV_NOINIT` region would never be initialized, unless the caller initializes it. So, that still doesn't explain the commit message issue!

The issue stems from the deferred initialization of the memory map (`CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT`). When this is enabled, the `struct page` structures are not fully initialized immediately during boot. This is done to improve boot time.

I traced the callers of `free_reserved_area()` and the calls made before `page_alloc_init_late()` to understand the timing of calls and determine if they were a concern. Calls from modules or sysfs via `reserve_mem_release_by_name()` were one concern, but those use a mutex, so there's less of a race condition there. Calls that free memory before the memory map is fully initialized are a concern. The critical window is between `kmem_cache_init()` (where slab allocator becomes available) and `page_alloc_init_late()`. I checked the EFI memory map, but it looks like the EFI memory map is typically set up early in `setup_arch()` before the problematic window.

The heart of the problem lies in buddy allocator's behavior. When freeing a page, the buddy allocator attempts to merge it with its adjacent buddy page. If the buddy page is within a region that's still being initialized, that buddy page might have invalid `struct page` contents, particularly the list pointers. These pointers are then accessed in `__free_one_page()` or `list_del()` when the page is being freed into the buddy allocator, causing a crash. This crash is triggered by `list_del(&buddy->lru)`, which fails if the buddy's `lru` entry is uninitialized.

The author claims the fix is to return zero, so we skip the `memblock_remove_range`. While this adds to a memory leak in the event of this race condition, it does prevent a crash. The author's check with `deferred_pages_enabled()` relies on `static_branch_unlikely()`. Since static keys are initialized early in the boot process, I confirmed that it's safe to use `static_branch_unlikely()`.

I also looked at other scenarios and if the check is in the right place. However, returning 0 means `memblock_remove_range` will still remove the pages from `memblock.reserved` but the pages are never added to the buddy.

The key point is that the uninitialized buddy page, due to deferred initialization, leads to this crash during the attempt to merge the pages in the buddy allocator.

The patch itself only adds the check in `__free_reserved_area()`. Thus if a function calls `free_reserved_page()` directly, it's still vulnerable.

However, if it's considered better to WARN and leak the memory rather than crash, it is a valid compromise.

    "thought": "**Deep Dive into Deferred Page Initialization**

Okay, let's break this down. First, the core concept: `deferred_init_memmap_chunk` initializes the memory map for *free* memory. The question then becomes, what about reserved memory? Initially, I thought reserved memory's `struct page` initialization was handled earlier during boot in `memmap_init_reserved_pages()`.

However, that's not quite right. Reserved pages *are* initialized before `deferred_init_memmap`. The crucial insight, as the commit message clarifies, is that freeing reserved memory *before* the full memory map initialization in `deferred_init_memmap()` causes problems accessing uninitialized `struct page` instances.

The mechanism is this:  `__free_reserved_area()` calls `free_reserved_page(page)`. `free_reserved_page()` sets page flags, potentially clears the reserved bit, and calls `free_unref_page` or `__free_page`. When a page is freed, it enters the buddy allocator. The buddy allocator's merging process depends on `page_is_buddy(page, buddy, order)`, which relies on accessing the `struct page` of the buddy. Crucially, the buddy could be a *free* page (i.e., not reserved), and thus, its `struct page` might still be deferred – uninitialized. If `page_is_buddy()` reads this uninitialized data, a crash could result. That's the core issue. Reserved pages can have free buddies, and those free buddies' `struct pages` are deferred, leading to an unsafe scenario before `deferred_init_memmap` completes.

The commit addresses this by returning `0` from `__free_reserved_area()` if `deferred_pages_enabled()` is true. This triggers a `WARN` and skips the `free_reserved_page()` loop.  Effectively, this means the memory is *leaked*. The comment explicitly states the goal is to prevent crashes from bad code moves or additions, and a leak is a tolerable outcome compared to a crash. This makes sense as a defensive measure.

Now, let's look at `deferred_pages_enabled()`.  It's defined using `CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT`. If that config option is set, it relies on a `static_branch_unlikely(&deferred_pages)`.

Hold on. `deferred_pages` is initialized as `TRUE` using `DECLARE_STATIC_KEY_TRUE(deferred_pages);`, but during normal operation of the kernel it's FALSE. It's disabled in `page_alloc_init_late()`. So, is it correct to use `static_branch_unlikely` on a key that starts as `TRUE`?  Yes, because `static_branch_unlikely` vs. `static_branch_likely` are hints to the compiler for branch prediction *in the steady state*.  While it begins true, it's expected to be false for most of the kernel's runtime, so using `static_branch_unlikely()` is a valid hint.

Finally, there's a minor code reorganization. The patch moves `deferred_pages_enabled()` from `mm/page_alloc.c` to `mm/internal.h`.  This is safe because `"internal.h"` is included by any `mm/page_alloc.c` files.

Okay, so looking at this, my initial reaction is *whoa*, there's a surprisingly high number of calls to `free_reserved_page()`! That immediately raises a flag. Now, the real issue I see is that direct calls from architecture-specific code – like in `arch/x86/mm/init_64.c`, `arch/arm64/...`, plus places like `mm/hugetlb.c`, `mm/cma.c`, and even `include/linux/kexec.h` – appear *unprotected* by the `__free_reserved_area` warning. That means if these calls happen too early in the boot process, we're staring down the barrel of a potential crash. This is a very serious concern.

Okay, so I've been reviewing this patch, and here's my take. The goal is to prevent a crash that occurs when reserved memory is prematurely freed, specifically before the memory map is fully initialized during early boot. The core issue revolves around the interaction of the buddy allocator with deferred page initialization.

Initially, I wanted to confirm includes, specifically if the required `<linux/jump_label.h>` was indirectly included, which seems likely. The patch focuses on a conditional check using `deferred_pages_enabled()`, guarding the `__free_reserved_area()` function. The underlying problem is that if `deferred_pages_enabled()` is true, and a page is freed, the buddy allocator can access uninitialized `struct page` data of its buddy pages because the memory map is not initialized for it. These uninitialized `struct page`s can contain garbage and in turn, cause issues during buddy page merging, specifically in `__list_del_entry_valid_or_report`, potentially leading to a page fault.

I confirmed that this is a valid defensive measure. The proposed patch checks the `deferred_pages_enabled()` state and, if true, issues a warning and returns 0, essentially leaking the memory. I identified that `free_reserved_page()` is actually the lower-level function that is vulnerable. The author likely put the guard in `__free_reserved_area()` instead of `free_reserved_page()` to avoid spamming the console with warnings, and to keep the "public API" call protected. It's a trade-off. Direct calls to `free_reserved_page()` are unprotected by this patch.

Also, I checked and confirmed that the check will always behave consistently, no matter how much memory the system has.

Finally, I checked when the vulnerable function is called, and although it seems that most callers run at the right time (after `page_alloc_init_late()`), there's a risk of an oversight. Okay, so I'm diving into this intricate dance of memory management during kernel initialization, specifically looking at the `memblock_phys_free` function and potential pitfalls. The core issue is this: a warning is triggered if `memblock_phys_free` is called at the *wrong* time – specifically, after the slab allocator is initialized (`slab_is_available()` is TRUE), but *before* the page allocator is fully set up (`page_alloc_init_late()` has run). This leads to a memory leak because the memory is removed from the reserved memblock, but *isn't* given to the buddy allocator, resulting in a permanent loss.

My journey started by tracing the call stack from `mm_core_init`, which is the function that initializes the slab allocator and sets `slab_is_available()` to TRUE. I found it's called early in `start_kernel()`. Then, `kernel_init_freeable` which eventually calls `page_alloc_init_late()` is called much later from `rest_init()`. The crucial timing window is the gap between `mm_core_init` returning and `page_alloc_init_late` running. During this window, any calls to `memblock_phys_free` will trigger the problematic scenario.

The underlying mechanism is this: `memblock_phys_free` calls `__free_reserved_area()` if the slab allocator is available.  However, `__free_reserved_area` will check `deferred_pages_enabled()` which is still enabled. When it sees TRUE, it prints a WARN, *and* returns 0 (which I later realized meant the memory isn't actually freed). Then, `memblock_phys_free` removes it from `memblock.reserved`, leading to the leak.

I realized that most early init functions run *before* `mm_core_init()`, so they're safe. After `mm_core_init()` runs, driver init functions run which also run after `page_alloc_init_late()` making them safe. The real danger is code that *happens* to run in this gap. The code is structured this way *on purpose*. The author *wants* to prevent memory deallocation into an uninitialized page allocator. It prevents potential crashes. The static key is used as a timing constraint.

I questioned if enabling `CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT` (which introduces `deferred_pages_enabled()`), but there are no actual deferred pages would cause problems. It doesn't, but the code correctly emits a warning. The correct interpretation is that the API rule is "don't call `free_reserved_area` before `page_alloc_init_late`", *period*.

Further, I noted that the side effect of returning 0 from `__free_reserved_area()` means `free_reserved_area` doesn't print a "Freeing memory" message. In `memblock_phys_free`, returning zero from `__free_reserved_area` means the memory is removed from the reserved block, but not sent to the buddy allocator leading to a permanent leak. This is the desired behavior for this forbidden window. The caller is broken and the warning is correct.

I also analyzed `free_reserved_page`, but found it doesn't call `__free_reserved_area`, making it exempt from this issue.

In essence, this analysis highlights a carefully crafted design to prevent memory corruption during the kernel's early boot phase. The warning mechanism is a deliberate measure to enforce this timing constraint and prevent premature memory deallocation into an uninitialized page allocator, even if no deferred pages are in the configuration.

More acutely, with CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT=y\n    efi_free_boot_services() is called before deferred initialization of the\n    memory map is complete.\n    \n    Benjamin Herrenschmidt     "thought": "**Analyzing the memblock_free() Vulnerability Window**

Okay, so I'm thinking about this issue concerning `memblock_free()` and the window between `kmem_cache_init()` and `page_alloc_init_late()`. My initial train of thought is to map out the function calls. First, I see that within `start_kernel()`, functions like `kmem_cache_init_late()` get called, along with `console_init()`, `lockdep_init()`, and a bunch of other early initialization routines. Then, `rest_init()` creates `kernel_init` and `kthreadd`. Inside `kernel_init_freeable()`, which is what `kernel_init` kthread runs, we get to things like `smp_prepare_cpus()`, `workqueue_init()`, and eventually `page_alloc_init_late()`.

The core of the problem, I believe, is that any function within this time frame that allocates memory using `memblock_alloc()` and attempts to free it using `memblock_free()` is going to trigger some unexpected behavior. Specifically, if memory is allocated via memblock and then later `memblock_free()` is called, we have a problem.

Now, let's step through the logic.  If `memblock_free()` is called, and `slab_is_available()` is TRUE, then `__free_reserved_area()` is called.  This function contains a check: `deferred_pages_enabled()`.  And now I see it, the added patch introduces a WARN and a return 0 if `deferred_pages_enabled()` is true. This means, if we're between `kmem_cache_init()` and `page_alloc_init_late()`, `deferred_pages_enabled()` *will* be true, and `__free_reserved_area` will fail to actually free the memory to the buddy allocator, resulting in a leak. The WARN serves as a warning, essentially saying that the caller is doing it wrong.

I'm starting to remember that there was some history here. I need to look into an old patch involving the removal of `memblock_free_late`. That's where things get interesting! The patch history shows that `memblock_free_late` got removed. So `memblock_free()` handles the job now. The earlier traces confirm this, which is great.

Okay, let's trace this logic out:

-   If `memblock_free` is called BEFORE `slab_is_available()`, then the memory gets removed from `memblock.reserved`, and the memory will be handled later. So memory is NOT leaked before `slab_is_available()`.
-   BUT, when `slab_is_available()` becomes true, that's when `memblock_free` calls `__free_reserved_area()`, and *this* is the problematic window where `deferred_pages_enabled()` is also true. The pages are removed but NOT freed, due to the intentional `WARN`.

So the critical question is, do we have any `memblock_free` calls during this window? The WARN was put there to catch them!  But I still worry because the memory is being *removed* from `memblock.reserved`!

Wait a second. What's the context here? `mm_core_init` is called early in `start_kernel()`, *before* `kmem_cache_init()`. Then, inside `mm_core_init()`, `mem_init()` gets called which calls `memblock_free_all()`. Now, `kmem_cache_init()` occurs *after* `mm_core_init()`, which means `memblock_free_all()` would have already cleared all *unreserved* memory to the buddy allocator.

So the window is: After `kmem_cache_init()` and before `page_alloc_init_late()`, but after `memblock_free_all()`. I must check for any calls in this window. The commit author added this WARN intentionally to CATCH such calls! And the memory is still leaked.

    "thought": "**Analysis of the Memory Leak Prevention Patch**

Okay, so I've been carefully examining this patch that introduces a warning and potential memory leak. The core of the issue revolves around calls to `memblock_free` (which eventually calls `__free_reserved_area`) occurring before `page_alloc_init_late()`. My initial concern stemmed from the fact that `__free_reserved_area` now includes a check using `deferred_pages_enabled()`. During the window between `kmem_cache_init()` and `page_alloc_init_late()`, this function evaluates to `TRUE`. The patch's introduction of `if (deferred_pages_enabled()) return 0;` within `__free_reserved_area` means pages are *not* freed to the buddy allocator, but `memblock_remove_range()` *does* remove them from the `memblock.reserved` list, resulting in a silent memory leak. I dug into the code, especially how `memblock_free` would work. My thought was there might be some odd timing involved here, and there wasn't.

Initially, I looked for code that calls `memblock_free` between the critical points. Code like `swiotlb_init` is called early, and there seems to be nothing major in the kernel. My first assumption was I could see problems where `free_reserved_page` and `__free_reserved_area` would interact. That wasn't the case here, given how the patch actually modifies `__free_reserved_area`. It also doesn't affect `free_reserved_page` which does not check `deferred_pages_enabled()`.

The commit message itself makes the intention clear: the warning and the "leak" are deliberate. The intent is to prevent a *crash*, and warn the user. The author assumes this situation is triggered by some caller that *shouldn't* be doing what it's doing.

The check for `deferred_pages_enabled()` is there because, before the full initialization of the memory map (`page_alloc_init_late()`), accessing the struct pages directly would be problematic or cause a crash. It all boils down to the fact that the actual struct pages have not been initialized yet. If we attempted to free reserved pages before initialization, things would go bad. The fix here is to essentially defer freeing, and warn the user, or potentially just leak (as per the author). This patch is meant to act as a safety net against incorrect code, but assumes the buggy code will be fixed. So the WARN is a signal of a caller that *needs* to be corrected, or is potentially doing something incorrect.

The `static_branch_unlikely()` call used by `deferred_pages_enabled()` relies on jump labels, and I confirmed that it's safe to use during early boot because the initialization of the jump labels happens early in `start_kernel()`. I needed to ensure this was safe, as well.

The author also notes in another commit that they did split `efi_free_boot_services` to first collect ranges to free, and then call them once the memory map is initialized.

It's clear that the design means it’s okay if `__free_reserved_area` returns early. It’s an intentional choice. I don’t see an obvious problem with this approach, given the intention to avoid corruption and ensure the caller gets a warning. The design also makes it very easy to find bad code.

    "thought": "**Analysis of `memblock_free()` and Deferred Page Initialization**

Okay, so I've been digging into this change, trying to wrap my head around this fix related to `memblock_free()`, deferred struct page initialization, and the warning that pops up. It seems like the core issue boils down to a race condition during early boot, specifically the window between `kmem_cache_init()` and `page_alloc_init_late()`.

The problem is that if `memblock_free()` is called in this window, we can trigger a warning and a memory leak. Here's the sequence: `memblock_free()` eventually calls `__free_reserved_area()`. Inside `__free_reserved_area()`, there's a check that prevents the actual freeing of memory if `deferred_pages_enabled()` returns true. This is the new guard.  `deferred_pages_enabled()` effectively means that the memory map isn't fully initialized and that freeing the pages right now could access uninitialized struct pages, causing a crash.

The key is that the warning is *intended*. The author isn't introducing a bug here but is creating a defensive mechanism to avoid crashing the kernel when someone mistakenly tries to free memory too early. Since the check prevents the memory from being freed, and then `memblock_remove_range()` is called, we get a memory leak because the pages are removed from `memblock.reserved`, but never given to the buddy allocator.

My initial thought was, "Hey, shouldn't we just return an error from `memblock_phys_free()` if we can't free the memory?" That's a valid thought, but it goes against the idea of preventing the crash. Returning an error could cause things to cascade. The current approach is, leak-and-warn: We leak and warn the developer.

I've checked to see what actually calls `memblock_free()` in the early boot process, looking for the potential culprits between `kmem_cache_init()` and `page_alloc_init_late()`. Turns out, it's not a lot. Initcalls could, however, trigger the issue. The new warning and intended leak *prevent* a crash that would've happened before, and, is a good defensive programming approach.

I also thought about the implementation itself. The use of `static_branch_unlikely` is correct and serves as an optimization hint for the compiler. The compiler will optimize for the FALSE case, as deferred initialization will eventually be disabled.

Now, my biggest concern was, is there any scenario where part of the memory map IS initialized, and this check is too aggressive?  Yes, because parts of memory are initialized at different points, some zones might be initialized *before* `page_alloc_init_late()` executes. That means a perfectly valid call might hit the warning and leak memory, because the check is too global, not specific to regions.

The key point here is, the warning is supposed to be in `__free_reserved_area()`, so this is why the issue of missing includes isn't relevant, since it's only called in one place in `memblock.c`. The new patch is correct: The warning is in the right place, and the leak is intentional.  The only downside is it may be more general than necessary. However, if code *was* already being called too early, before this patch the code would have crashed, so this is an improvement. The kernel will now WARN, not crash. The kernel's behavior is consistent, by design.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Implications of `free_reserved_page` and Deferred Pages**

Okay, so I'm looking at how `free_reserved_page` interacts with the deferred pages functionality, and this is more complex than I initially thought. I see that `free_reserved_page` is called from various places, including module initialization and even during early boot on some architectures. This requires a careful analysis to ensure memory isn't corrupted.

The commit author added a check for deferred pages, but only in `__free_reserved_area`, not in `free_reserved_page` itself, which seems odd. Why not? `free_reserved_page` is exported and used by modules. I had to go digging through the code. I see that the commit moved `deferred_pages_enabled()` *out* of `mm/page_alloc.c`, so now it's in `<mm/internal.h>`.  I think I know why now. The commit author needs the guard at a higher level than the leaf function, `free_reserved_page()`, in order to correctly guard all calls.

The `mm/page_alloc.c` implementation of `free_reserved_page` is straightforward, so I'm thinking, why not put the `deferred_pages_enabled()` check directly in `free_reserved_page`? That would protect *all* callers! But if you did that, the function returns `void`, so how would it signal an error? It would have to WARN and return early. However, `__free_reserved_area` handles a region of pages, so if you WARN on every single page, it would spam the logs. Putting the check in `__free_reserved_area` is better because it prints the warning only once for the whole region. It's an intelligent design choice.

I had to look at how the memory is actually freed. I see that the call `memblock_phys_free` gets hit. `memblock_free_late` was removed. `memblock_phys_free` calls `__free_reserved_area`, which is protected. That covers the generic case well. I am concerned about other architectures calling `free_reserved_page` during early boot, but it's likely that a crash there would be caught during testing.

Then I wondered, would it be better to add the `WARN_ON_ONCE` within `free_reserved_page()` itself? But it appears the author thought, failing early in `__free_reserved_area` is sufficient. I think the issue with that is now `memblock_remove_range` is called even when a deferred page free is detected. If `__free_reserved_area` returns zero, `memblock_remove_range` still runs. This can cause a silent memory leak where the memory isn't tracked by memblock, even though it wasn't returned to the buddy allocator.

My thought is, it would be *better* to keep this memory in the memblock reserved list. That correctly indicates it's still reserved, and not free, at that point. But then I realized, once the buddy allocator is up, `memblock` is barely used. I am right in thinking about kdump and hibernation.

Hibernate uses the page allocator, and if those pages aren't in buddy, they are considered allocated, right? So hibernation will still save those pages, even if they're "leaked." If they aren't in `memblock.reserved`, and they are NOT in buddy, I need to look at if struct pages exist. The struct pages haven't been initialized yet, and they *will* be initialized by `deferred_init_memmap()`.

Then I see, `deferred_init_memmap()` assumes that any pages NOT in `memblock.reserved` are considered FREE. And I see that `deferred_init_memmap` calls `init_deferred_page()`, which sets the page count to zero, and marks them as free. This is a bit unsettling! So, it looks like a subtle trade-off was made here: a potential memory leak vs. potentially spamming the log.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Free Reserved Memory Patch**

Okay, so the author's rationale seems to be about adding a safety net. They're worried about future code changes or additions that might call `free_reserved_area()` (which is called by `memblock_phys_free()`, formerly `memblock_free_late()`) at the wrong time – specifically, before the memory map is fully initialized. The patch adds a warning in `__free_reserved_area()` to prevent this.

But, hang on... the author's approach is a bit… selective. The commit message mentions an attempt to call `free_reserved_page()` too early. The code specifically puts the check inside `__free_reserved_area()`. Why there and not in `free_reserved_page()` itself? There are *lots* of callers of `free_reserved_page()` like `free_bootmem_page()`, balloon, kexec, hugetlb, and CMA. Are all these direct calls happening at the “right timings”? The author seems to have focused primarily on `free_reserved_area()` and its indirect caller `memblock_phys_free()`.

If the check were in `free_reserved_page()`, we'd be looking at potentially thousands of warnings. So, they probably considered using `WARN_ON_ONCE` or maybe `WARN(1, ...)` and returning early. But then, if we return early, we *skip* the free, which isn't ideal. The choice of `__free_reserved_area()` means the bulk free APIs (`memblock_phys_free()`, `free_reserved_area()`) are protected but what about a *direct* call to `free_reserved_page()`? It bypasses the check! That's a real issue. The commit itself mentions this problem, it's not a complete protection. An "API omission".

This leads to the next concern: The global `deferred_pages_enabled()` check is unconditionally blocking any freeing before `page_alloc_init_late()`. I need to understand this better. `deferred_pages_enabled()` relies on `static_branch_unlikely(&deferred_pages)`. It looks like it is `TRUE` initially! When is `deferred_pages` ever set to `FALSE`? This needs deeper investigation. I'm wondering if this "protection" is actually causing early memory leaks by blocking valid frees before deferred initialization completes.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the `memblock_phys_free` Warning in Early Boot**

Okay, here's what I'm thinking about this patch: It all centers around this warning that gets triggered when `memblock_phys_free()` (or, in practice, a lower-level call like `free_bootmem_page` which eventually uses `memblock_phys_free`) is called too early in the boot process. Specifically, before the memory map is fully initialized via `page_alloc_init_late()`.

Initially, I noted the timing: `mm_core_init` happens, then `kmem_cache_init_late`, and *way* later, `page_alloc_init_late` in `kernel_init`.  There's a window between `slab_is_available()` (available after `kmem_cache_init`) and `page_alloc_init_late()` where a bunch of initialization code runs. This includes `do_pre_smp_initcalls()`, but importantly, `do_basic_setup()` and regular `initcalls` happen *after* `page_alloc_init_late()`.

The crucial point is that during this window, `deferred_pages_enabled()` is true. This means the system is still in a deferred initialization state for the memory allocator.  If `memblock_phys_free()` is called here, it will trigger the warning and, crucially, *leak* the memory.

My first thought was, "Is there any *valid* reason to free reserved memory during this timeframe?" The author seems to think not, adding the warning as a guardrail.  They mention that the current callers appear to be fine – meaning, they happen *after* `page_alloc_init_late()`. But the warning is there to *prevent* future code moves or additions from causing trouble.

This leads to the heart of the concerns: The "Overly Broad Restriction" because it could cause memory leaks for *valid* early frees, and "State Inconsistency" for the same reason.  But here's the twist:  The author, Mike Rapoport, is essentially setting a *policy*.  He's saying "don't free reserved memory until deferred init is done."  Even if the memory *is* technically initialized, the global `deferred_pages_enabled()` flag doesn't check for that fine grain of detail.  He's enforcing a blanket policy, even if it might leak in some hypothetical, perfectly-valid but early-freeing scenario.

The crucial bit, revealed in the commit message, is that this is *motivated* by a real-world problem in x86 EFI code.  This patch *will* now make that EFI code leak memory if it's freeing things too early.  But it's probably an *improvement*! It's trading a potential crash (as the commit mentions) for a warning and leak.  The warning is meant to signal that the EFI code needs to be fixed. It's a *defensive fallback*.

Now, the question is, is this a valid policy to set? Is there valid use case to free a low-memory page early? The maintainer says no, and this patch enforces that. So, Concerns 2 and 3 ("Overly Broad Restriction" and "State Inconsistency") are actually describing the *intentional* defensive behavior of the patch, not a bug.

What about the safety check in `__free_reserved_area()` not protecting direct callers of `free_reserved_page()`? Well, a previous commit changed `memblock_free_late` to `memblock_phys_free`. This means that `memblock_phys_free` IS protected. Then other direct callers are: `free_bootmem_page`, `free_reserved_ptdesc`, and some other kernel subsystems. I will have to look at `free_bootmem_page` to check why early calls were causing issues.

memblock: warn when freeing reserved memory before memory map is initialized

This commit adds a warning when attempting to free reserved memory before the
memory map is fully initialized by deferred_init_memmap(). This aims to
prevent access to uninitialized struct pages which can cause crashes.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/e5d5a1105d90ee1e7fe7eafaed2ed03bbad0c46b.camel@kernel.crashing.org/

> diff --git a/mm/memblock.c b/mm/memblock.c
> index bd5758ff07f2..780e70d4971a 100644
> --- a/mm/memblock.c
> +++ b/mm/memblock.c
> @@ -896,6 +896,11 @@ static unsigned long __free_reserved_area(phys_addr_t start, phys_addr_t end,
>  {
>  	unsigned long pages = 0, pfn;
>  
> +	if (deferred_pages_enabled()) {
> +		WARN(1, "Cannot free reserved memory because of deferred initialization of the memory map");
> +		return 0;
> +	}
> +

Does this global check block freeing memory that is already fully
initialized, such as memory in ZONE_DMA or ZONE_NORMAL?

Since __free_reserved_area() returns 0 when this triggers, the pages are
not given to the buddy allocator. However, the caller memblock_phys_free()
does not check this return value and proceeds to call
memblock_remove_range(). Does this result in a permanent memory leak for
early valid frees?

Would it be better to check if the specific PFN range is actually deferred,
rather than applying a global restriction?

Could this also leave direct callers of free_reserved_page() unprotected?

For example, architecture-specific initialization code (like in
arch/sparc/kernel/leon_smp.c) might call free_reserved_page() during early
boot before page_alloc_init_late().

Would it be safer to place this validation directly inside
free_reserved_page() to ensure all paths are covered?

>  		struct page *page = pfn_to_page(pfn);

```text
> index bd5758ff07f2..780e70d4971a 100644
> --- a/mm/memblock.c
> +++ b/mm/memblock.c
> @@ -896,6 +896,11 @@ static unsigned long __free_reserved_area(phys_addr_t start, phys_addr_t end,
>  {
>  	unsigned long pages = 0, pfn;
>  
> +	if (deferred_pages_enabled()) {
> +		WARN(1, "Cannot free reserved memory because of deferred initialization of the memory map");
> +		return 0;
> +	}
> +
```

Does this global check block freeing memory that is already fully
initialized, such as memory in ZONE_DMA or ZONE_NORMAL?

Since `__free_reserved_area()` returns 0 when this triggers, the pages are
not given to the buddy allocator. However, the caller `memblock_phys_free()`
does not check this return value and proceeds to call
`memblock_remove_range()`. Does this result in a permanent memory leak for
early valid frees?

Would it be better to check if the specific PFN range is actually deferred,
rather than applying a global restriction?

Could this also leave direct callers of `free_reserved_page()` unprotected?

For example, architecture-specific initialization code (like in
`arch/sparc/kernel/leon_smp.c`) might call `free_reserved_page()` during early
boot before `page_alloc_init_late()`.

Would it be safer to place this validation directly inside
`free_reserved_page()` to ensure all paths are covered?

```
>  	for_each_valid_pfn(pfn, PFN_UP(start), PFN_DOWN(end)) {
>  		struct page *page = pfn_to_page(pfn);
>  		void *direct_map_addr;
```
It looks like this patch puts the check in `__free_reserved_area()`. My concern is whether this is the best place for it.

Author: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>

memblock: warn when freeing reserved memory before memory map is initialized

This commit adds a warning when attempting to free reserved memory before the
memory map is fully initialized by deferred_init_memmap(). This aims to
prevent access to uninitialized struct pages which can cause crashes.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/e5d5a1105d90ee1e7fe7eafaed2ed03bbad0c46b.camel@kernel.crashing.org/
